exemplar.title: Prompt 26: âDoes our knowledge depend on our interactions with other knowers?â

detailedExemplarData.title: Prompt 26: âDoes our knowledge depend on our interactions with other knowers?â

strengths: Clear identification of three specific real-world objects and their authentic contexts (family marble chessboard, Newtonâs personal copy of Principia, ITER cutaway diagram).
Explicit connections made between each object and IA Prompt 26, with commentary on how social interaction (or its absence) influences knowledge production and transfer.
Concrete examples support many points (chess lessons with grandfather, Newtonâs reliance on Galileo and Kepler, ITERâs 35-nation consortium).
Effective use of visual materials (photographs and diagrams) to reinforce object authenticity and grounding in real contexts.
Inclusion of a compelling counterexample (Newtonâs isolation during the plague) and a thoughtful counterclaim (learning bot as a knower) that deepen the engagement with the promptâs tension.
weaknesses: Explanations occasionally lack depth and precision, with some analysis drifting into description rather than sustained, object-specific insight.
Frequent repetition of broad assertions across objects instead of developing distinct justifications for each.
Uneven use of core TOK terminology and conceptual frameworks (ways of knowing, knowledge frameworks), weakening theoretical rigor.
Limited critical balance for ITER (no counter-perspective) and a conclusion that restates earlier points without new analysis or reflection.
Inconsistent citation style with minimal references and missing bibliography, undermining the academic rigor of the exhibition.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work meets the expectations of the 5â6 band by clearly identifying three objects, situating them in real contexts, and making explicit links to the chosen IA prompt with reasonable justification. It demonstrates adequate engagement through concrete examples and some critical insight (e.g., Newtonâs counterexample, bot as knower). However, the analysis lacks consistent precision, depth and use of TOK concepts; there is repetition across objects and insufficient citation rigor. These shortcomings position the exhibition at the lower end of the 5â6 band, warranting a score of 5.

annotations.comment: The student effectively uses Newtonâs isolation during the plague as a counterexample to illustrate the potential for knowledge creation without direct interaction, engaging with the promptâs tension.
The inclusion of a detailed photograph reinforces the objectâs authenticity and supports the studentâs justification by providing a clear visual reference.

The student clearly identifies a specific real-world object (the family chessboard) and situates it authentically within their personal context, meeting the requirement to ground the exhibition in concrete examples.
No counter-perspective is offered for ITER (e.g., risks of centralized knowledge), which limits critical balance in the analysis.
This paragraph ends mid-thought, leaving the argument unresolved and undermining the coherence of the exhibitionâs conclusion.
The single footnote uses an inconsistent citation style; a full bibliography and uniform referencing format are required for academic rigor.
The student introduces a thoughtful counterclaim by questioning whether a learning bot qualifies as a knower, adding depth to the exploration of knowledge dependence on interpersonal interaction.
Including an image of Newtonâs original title page strengthens the real-world authenticity and underpins the link to the exhibitionâs prompt.
The anecdote about the apple myth is presented without source or critical evaluation, perpetuating a popular legend rather than grounding the claim in historical evidence.
Despite the strong counterexample, there is no explicit mention of TOK methods or ways of knowing to clarify how Newtonâs reasoning fits within a conceptual framework.
Linking ITERâs 35-nation consortium directly to the prompt demonstrates a strong justification for how collective interaction produces novel knowledge.
The justification for the chessboardâs inclusion repeats ideas rather than deepening the analysis, resulting in a formulaic explanation that adds little new insight.
Historical assertions (e.g., Newtonâs academic background) are unsupported by citations or references, weakening the evidential basis of the analysis.
The detailed 3D cutaway diagram of ITER effectively conveys the objectâs complexity and underlines its real-world significance.

This narrative description is largely anecdotal and lacks explicit engagement with TOK terminology (such as âways of knowingâ or âknowledge frameworksâ), reducing the conceptual precision of the analysis.
The paragraph ends abruptly with an incomplete sentence, disrupting coherence and leaving the argument underdeveloped.
Although the prompt is stated, the student seldom uses core TOK concepts to frame their discussion, weakening the exhibitionâs theoretical grounding.
The student precisely names Newtonâs Principia Mathematica and provides publication details, effectively situating the second object in its historical context.
The synthesis of how Newtonâs later peer engagement underscores the prompt adds valuable depth, linking solitary reflection back to communal validation.
The student precisely titles Object 3 as the ITER diagram, clearly identifying the object and its intended source.
The claim that ITERâs research collaboration dates back to WW1 is historically inaccurateâfusion science began mid-20th centuryâand requires precise chronology or correction.
Referencing JET and NIF research provides additional concrete examples of collaborative knowledge production, enriching the exhibitionâs evidential base.
The conclusion largely restates earlier points without offering new analysis or reflecting on implications for knowledge theory, limiting intellectual growth.

Throughout the exhibition, the student would benefit from explicitly integrating core TOK concepts (such as ways of knowing, areas of knowledge and knowledge frameworks) to deepen theoretical analysis.
A comprehensive bibliography listing all sources cited (beyond the single footnote) should be included to support transparency and academic integrity.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

The exhibition presents
three objects, but the
real-world contexts of
these objects are not
stated, or the images
presented may be
highly generic images of
types of object rather
than of specific
real-world objects. Links
between the objects
and the selected IA
prompt are made, but
these are minimal,
tenuous, or it is not
clear what the student is
trying to convey.
There is very little
justification offered for
the inclusion of each
object in the exhibition.
The commentary on the
objects is highly
descriptive or consists
only of unsupported
assertions.

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.
